cheatsheet:
Sequences
- Sequence can be iterated once with for-in
- Collection adds indices and count
- lazy creates deferred transformations
description: Implement a custom iterator by conforming to `IteratorProtocol` and manually managing its state through the `next()` method.
hints: The 'IteratorProtocol' requires a single method: 'mutating func next() -> T?'.
The 'next()' method returns the next element in the sequence, or 'nil' when the sequence ends.
The iterator needs to track its internal state (like a 'current' value) to know what to return next.
Update your 'current' value before returning it, or use 'defer' to increment it after the return.
title: Custom Iterator

cheatsheet:
Sequences
- Sequence can be iterated once with for-in
- Collection adds indices and count
- lazy creates deferred transformations
description: Convert a custom sequence into a concrete array to access collection-specific properties like `count` and `last`.
hints: A 'Sequence' only guarantees that it can be iterated; it doesn't necessarily know its count or support random access.
To access these features, you can 'materialize' the sequence by passing it to an Array initializer: 'Array(sequence)'.
The 'items' array will contain all elements produced by the sequence's iterator.
Print the count and the last element of the resulting array.
lesson:
Lesson: Materializing Sequences
A 'Sequence' is an abstract representation of data that can be traversed. Some sequences might even be infinite! Because of this, sequences don't always support properties like 'count' or subscripts like '[0]'.

Core Concepts:
1. Sequence vs Collection: A 'Sequence' is the most basic protocol for iteration. A 'Collection' (like Array or Dictionary) builds on top of it, adding indices, counts, and stable multi-pass iteration.
2. Conversion: You can convert any finite sequence into an array using 'Array(sequence)'. This is called 'materializing' the sequence because it stores all the values in memory.
3. Access: Once materialized, you can use all the familiar array methods and properties.

Example:
    let range = 1...5
    let array = Array(range) // Materializes the range into [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
    print(array.count)

Note: Be careful when materializing very large or infinite sequences, as this can consume all available memory.
title: Sequence vs Collection

cheatsheet:
Actors
- actor isolates mutable state
- actor methods are accessed with await
- @MainActor marks main-thread-bound work
description: Compose multiple advanced Swift features—async/await, actors, and property wrappers—to build a thread-safe system with validated data.
hints: The '@Clamped' property wrapper ensures that the 'heat' property stays within the 0 to 2000 range.
Accessing or modifying 'furnace.heat' from outside the actor must be done asynchronously using 'await'.
Call 'addHeat(2200)' inside the 'runAsync' block; the wrapper will automatically clamp it to 2000.
Print the resulting heat value to verify the integration of all components.
title: Integration Challenge

cheatsheet:
Advanced Generics
- some returns an opaque type
- any uses an existential type
- Type erasure hides concrete types
- where clauses add constraints
description: Return an opaque type with some
hints: Return a concrete type that conforms to Metal.
Use the name property when printing.
lesson:
Lesson: Advanced Generics
Advanced generics express relationships between types.

Overview:
- Associated types describe placeholder types in protocols.
- `where` clauses constrain combinations of generic types.
- Protocol extensions can add constrained default behavior.

Example:
    protocol Store {
        associatedtype Item
        mutating func add(_ item: Item)
    }

Guidelines:
- Encode real constraints in the type system.
- Prefer simpler signatures unless constraints add clear value.
title: Opaque Types

cheatsheet:
Performance
- Arrays use copy-on-write behavior
- MemoryLayout reports size/stride/alignment
- Measure before optimizing
hints: Arrays copy on write when mutated.
Update second, then print both counts.
lesson:
Lesson: Performance
Optimize with measurement first, then targeted changes.

Overview:
- Reduce repeated allocations and unnecessary copies.
- Avoid repeated work inside loops.
- Prefer linear passes over nested scans when possible.

Example:
- Cache derived values when reused.
- Use `Set` for frequent membership checks.

Guidelines:
- Keep code correct first.
- Verify gains with realistic inputs.
solution:
var first = [1, 2]
var second = first

second.append(3)

print(first.count)
print(second.count)
title: Copy-on-Write

cheatsheet:
Performance
- Arrays use copy-on-write behavior
- MemoryLayout reports size/stride/alignment
- Measure before optimizing
hints: MemoryLayout<T>.size returns the size in bytes.
Use the same pattern for both types.
lesson:
Lesson: Advanced Memory
Memory behavior affects performance and correctness in larger systems.

Overview:
- Understand value vs reference semantics.
- Watch ARC churn in high-frequency paths.
- Avoid retain cycles in closure-heavy code.

Example:
- Capture `[weak self]` in escaping closures tied to object lifetime.
- Prefer value types for independent snapshots.

Guidelines:
- Measure before micro-optimizing.
- Choose ownership rules that are explicit and simple.
title: MemoryLayout

cheatsheet:
Performance
- Arrays use copy-on-write behavior
- MemoryLayout reports size/stride/alignment
- Measure before optimizing
hints: Use string interpolation to show the elapsed time.
Print the total first, then the elapsed line.
lesson:
Lesson: Performance
Optimize with measurement first, then targeted changes.

Overview:
- Reduce repeated allocations and unnecessary copies.
- Avoid repeated work inside loops.
- Prefer linear passes over nested scans when possible.

Example:
- Cache derived values when reused.
- Use `Set` for frequent membership checks.

Guidelines:
- Keep code correct first.
- Verify gains with realistic inputs.
solution:
import Foundation

var total = 0
for value in 1...100_000 {
    total += value
}
let elapsed = 0.0

print("Total: \(total)")
print(String(format: "Elapsed: %.2f", elapsed))
title: Profiling Mindset

cheatsheet:
Advanced Features
- Custom operators declare precedence
- Subscripts can accept multiple parameters
- dynamicMemberLookup forwards unknown members
- dynamicCallable forwards calls
hints: Use infix operator and a matching function implementation.
The operator function should return an Int.
lesson:
Lesson: Advanced Features
Advanced Swift features improve expressiveness when used deliberately.

Overview:
- Compose protocols, generics, and builders for clear APIs.
- Keep abstractions small and testable.
- Favor explicit data flow over clever indirection.

Example:
- Build a mini DSL with result builders.
- Layer protocol-driven components with dependency injection.

Guidelines:
- Introduce abstraction only when it reduces duplication.
- Prefer clarity over novelty.
title: Custom Operators

description: Review how macro usage looks in Swift
hints: Macros expand at compile time.
Output a short summary of what macros do.
lesson:
Lesson: Macros
Macros generate Swift code at compile time.

Overview:
- Use macros to remove repetitive boilerplate.
- Prefer small, predictable expansions.
- Keep generated behavior obvious to readers.

Example:
- Attached macros can add members or conformances.
- Freestanding macros can transform expressions/declarations.

Guidelines:
- Validate generated output during development.
- Avoid macros when a simple function solves the problem.
title: Macros (Usage)

cheatsheet:
SwiftPM
- Package.swift defines targets and dependencies
- Executable targets produce CLI tools
- Library targets expose modules
hints: Package.swift lists executable and test targets.
Report the target names in a single line.
lesson:
Lesson: SwiftPM
Swift Package Manager defines how modules build and depend on each other.

Overview:
- `Package.swift` declares products, targets, and dependencies.
- Targets control source layout and resource inclusion.
- Build configs and flags tune behavior by environment.

Example:
- Add a library dependency and link it in a target.
- Keep package boundaries small and purposeful.

Guidelines:
- Favor simple package graphs.
- Keep public APIs stable across package boundaries.
title: SwiftPM Basics
